race.name: Death Ride
race.display_name: Death Ride — Tour of the California Alps
race.tagline: 5 mountain passes. 14,000 feet of climbing. The Sierra Nevada's ultimate single-day climbing challenge since 1978.

vitals.registration: Online via bikereg.com. Sells out. Cost: ~$140-175
vitals.aid_stations: Support stations at each pass summit and base. Water, food, mechanics.
vitals.cutoff_time: Must return to Turtle Rock Park by 3:00 PM. Self-paced within cutoff.
distance_options: Full Death Ride: 103 miles, 14,000+ ft — 3 passes (6 summits, up and back)
Riders can turn around at any pass for partial completion

climate.primary: Sierra Nevada summer — hot valleys, cold summits
climate.description: July in the Sierra Nevada brings warm valley temperatures (80-95F) with significantly cooler conditions at pass summits (8,000+ ft). The 5:00 AM start means riding in cold darkness initially. Afternoon heat in exposed canyons is intense.
challenges: Temperature range from near-freezing at dawn to 90F+ in afternoon canyons
High-altitude sun exposure — intense UV above 7,000 ft
Thin air above 8,000 ft affects performance
Afternoon heat in exposed Sierra canyons

terrain.surface: Paved mountain roads (California state highways). Roads are open to traffic but well-supported on event day.
features: Monitor Pass (Hwy 89) — sustained climb to 8,314 ft
Ebbetts Pass (Hwy 4) — steep, winding, rarely traveled mountain road
Pacific Grade (Hwy 4) — additional summit at 8,050 ft
All passes ridden up and back — 6 total summits
Sustained riding between 5,500 ft and 8,732 ft elevation

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: The California Alps' defining climbing challenge since 1978. 5 passes, 14,000 ft of climbing in one of America's least-populated counties. T2 by score (71). p4 cannot promote into T1.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Elevation=5 and altitude=4 reflect the massive climbing volume and genuine high-altitude riding. Experience=5 for the achievement of completing all passes. Technicality=1 (all paved). cultural_impact=3 for American cycling institution status.

logistics.explanation: Markleeville is one of the most remote start locations for any major US cycling event — Alpine County has a population under 1,200. Getting there requires a 2+ hour drive from Reno or Sacramento over mountain roads. Accommodation is extremely limited (camping and a few lodges). The organization manages 3,500 riders across remote mountain passes, which is impressive, but the remoteness is a genuine logistics challenge.

length.explanation: 103 miles with 14,000+ feet of climbing is a serious single-day effort. The up-and-back format on each pass means you're constantly climbing or descending — there's minimal flat riding. The 5:00 AM start to 3:00 PM cutoff creates genuine time pressure. Most riders take 8-12 hours for the full route.

technicality.explanation: Paved California highways. The descents from Ebbetts Pass are winding and steep but on good asphalt. The challenge is climbing, not technical terrain.

elevation.explanation: 14,000+ feet of climbing across 5 passes (6 summits counting both directions). Three HC-category climbs back to back. This is among the most climbing packed into any single-day US cycling event. The up-and-back format means every foot of elevation is earned twice.

climate.explanation: July Sierra Nevada weather is generally favorable — sunny and dry. But the temperature range from the cold pre-dawn start to hot afternoon canyons requires layering strategy. High-altitude UV is intense. Not as extreme as La Marmotte's Galibier conditions, but the combination of heat and altitude creates real physiological stress.

altitude.explanation: Sustained riding between 5,500 ft and 8,732 ft. Pass summits above 8,000 ft deliver meaningful altitude effects — reduced oxygen, higher heart rate, slower recovery. Riders from sea level feel this acutely. The cumulative effect across 5 passes compounds the altitude impact.

adventure.explanation: Alpine County is wilderness. One of the least-populated counties in California — entirely undeveloped Sierra Nevada country. The Ebbetts Pass road is rarely traveled; on Death Ride day cyclists essentially own it. The Sierra Nevada scenery is spectacular — granite peaks, alpine meadows, rushing rivers. The remoteness is part of the appeal.

prestige.explanation: Nearly 50 years of history as the defining high-altitude road climbing event in the American West. Completing all 5 passes earns genuine respect in US cycling circles. The name alone carries weight. Not quite the international prestige of La Marmotte, but within US road cycling, the Death Ride is a bucket-list achievement.

race_quality.explanation: Solid organization for a remote mountain event. Aid stations at each pass summit and base with food, water, and mechanics. Medical support along the route. But this is not a professional sportive — it's a community cycling challenge run by Alpine County locals. The atmosphere is more grassroots than corporate.

experience.explanation: Completing 5 passes in the California Alps is a profound cycling experience. The pre-dawn start, the silent climb through darkness, the first summit as the sun rises, the rhythm of climb-descend-climb-descend, and the final return to Markleeville with all passes checked off — it's a day that stays with you. The Sierra Nevada scenery at altitude, the challenge of the climbing, and the achievement of finishing create a complete experience.

community.explanation: 3,500 riders create a shared challenge, but the spread across 103 miles of mountain roads means you're often riding alone or in small groups. The Markleeville start/finish area has a warm, small-town atmosphere. Many riders are Death Ride veterans who return year after year. The community is dedicated but not the 10,000-person festival of RAGBRAI or Flanders.

field_depth.explanation: Not competitive. There are no timing chips, no age group awards, no podium. The Death Ride is a personal challenge — you versus the passes. Some fast riders push for time, but it's unofficial. The field is self-selecting: 3,500 people willing to climb 14,000 feet in a day are serious cyclists by definition.

value.explanation: $140-175 for a single-day climbing event is moderate. The support is good but not lavish. The value is in the experience and the achievement rather than event perks.

expenses.explanation: Getting to Markleeville is expensive. Flights to Reno or Sacramento, then a 2+ hour drive. Accommodation is extremely limited — camping or the few lodges in Alpine County. You're self-sufficient in a remote mountain area. Budget $600-1,200 for travel, lodging, and event.

biased_opinion.summary: The Death Ride is American road cycling's ultimate climbing challenge. 5 passes. 14,000 feet. One of the least-populated counties in California. No prize money, no timing chips, no Instagram-ready finish line — just you and the Sierra Nevada. Since 1978, serious cyclists have made the pilgrimage to Markleeville to test themselves against the California Alps. The name isn't hyperbole — it's a warning and a promise. If you finish all 5 passes, you've earned something that can't be bought.
strengths: 14,000+ feet of climbing: among the most in any single-day US cycling event
Sierra Nevada wilderness: remote, beautiful, and genuinely wild
The achievement: completing all 5 passes carries permanent cycling credibility
Nearly 50 years of tradition — a genuine American cycling institution
weaknesses: Remote: Markleeville is 2+ hours from the nearest city. Accommodation is minimal.
Not competitive: no timing, no awards. If you need racing, look elsewhere.
Altitude: riders from sea level will feel the 8,000+ ft summits
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want to climb everything in a single California day, the Death Ride is waiting. It's been waiting since 1978.

final_verdict.one_liner: 5 passes. 14,000 feet. The California Alps' ultimate climbing challenge since 1978.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want the defining high-altitude climbing experience in the American West. Yes if you can handle sustained riding above 8,000 ft. Skip if you need race infrastructure or a competitive field.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want more climbing with international prestige: try La Marmotte. If you want US climbing with a race atmosphere: try Levi's GranFondo Growler. If you want multi-pass alpine challenge in Europe: try Tour du Mont-Blanc Cyclo.

logistics.airport: Reno-Tahoe (RNO) — 2 hours. Sacramento (SMF) — 3 hours. Both require mountain driving.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Extremely limited. Markleeville has a few lodges and campgrounds. Many riders camp at Turtle Rock Park. Book months ahead or commute from South Lake Tahoe (45 min).
logistics.food: Limited options in Markleeville — bring supplies. Post-ride BBQ at Turtle Rock Park.
logistics.packet_pickup: Friday at Turtle Rock Park. Small-town atmosphere.
logistics.parking: Turtle Rock Park. Arrive early — 3,500 riders fill the area.

history.founder: Five cyclists who rode the California Alps informally; formalized circa 1981
history.origin_story: In 1978, five cyclists rode the mountain passes of Alpine County in a single day. The ride grew organically into an organized event, formalized around 1981. Nearly 50 years later, the Death Ride draws 3,500 riders to one of California's most remote counties for what has become the defining high-altitude cycling challenge in the American West.
notable_moments: 1978: Five cyclists complete the original ride
Grew from informal challenge to America's premier climbing event
3,500 riders annually in one of California's least-populated counties
Proceeds benefit Alpine County community organizations
history.reputation: The climbing test. America's answer to the European mountain passes.

course_description.character: Relentless climbing. The up-and-back format on each pass means you're constantly ascending or descending. There is almost no flat riding. The passes come in waves — each summit brings the descent that takes you to the base of the next climb. By pass 4, the cumulative altitude exposure and climbing fatigue make every gradient feel steeper.

suffering_zones.label: Pre-Dawn Start
suffering_zones.desc: 5:00 AM departure from Turtle Rock Park in darkness. Cold, quiet, and humbling. The first pass awaits.

suffering_zones.label: Monitor Pass
suffering_zones.desc: The first major summit at 8,314 ft. Sustained climbing at altitude. The Sierra Nevada reveals itself as the sun rises. Descend back, then head for Ebbetts.

suffering_zones.label: Ebbetts Pass
suffering_zones.desc: The steepest and most dramatic climb. A rarely traveled Sierra Nevada road that winds through granite canyons. Summit at 8,732 ft — the highest point of the day. On tired legs at altitude, this is where the Death Ride earns its name.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Hwy 4, summit 8,732 ft, steep and winding

suffering_zones.label: Pacific Grade — The Final Summit
suffering_zones.desc: The last pass. Legs are empty, altitude has accumulated, and the clock is ticking toward the 3:00 PM cutoff. Summit at 8,050 ft. The descent back to Markleeville is the release.

course_description.signature_challenge: Ebbetts Pass — the steepest, most dramatic, and highest summit. Climbing to 8,732 ft on tired legs in the Sierra Nevada is the Death Ride's defining moment.

guide_variables.race_name: Death Ride — Tour of the California Alps
guide_variables.race_weather: Hot valleys (80-95F), cool summits (8,000+ ft), pre-dawn cold start
race_challenges: 14,000 feet of climbing across 5 passes in a single day
Altitude effects above 8,000 ft — reduced oxygen
Temperature swings from pre-dawn cold to afternoon canyon heat

non_negotiables.requirement: Mountain climbing endurance — back-to-back pass simulations
non_negotiables.why: 5 passes demand climbing-specific endurance that flat-land training can't replicate. If you don't have mountains, use sustained threshold efforts to simulate.

non_negotiables.requirement: Altitude exposure or preparation for riding above 8,000 ft
non_negotiables.why: Sea-level riders lose 10-15% performance at 8,000 ft. Arrive early to acclimatize or use altitude training preparation.

non_negotiables.requirement: Pacing plan with 3:00 PM cutoff reverse-engineered
non_negotiables.why: The cutoff is real. Calculate your required pace for each pass and stick to it. Starting too fast leads to bonking on pass 4.

youtube_data.search_query: Death Ride gran fondo cycling Markleeville

videos.title: Death Ride Documentary Demo Segment
videos.transcript:
A few miles from Markleyville, riders
reach the junction of Highway 89. The
skeleton squad directs them to the left
to begin the first climb. The front and
back side of Monitor Pass makes up
approximately 28 mi of the course.
The support for this portion of the
course is incredible with two water
stops and two full rest
[Music]
stops. Monitor is a tough climb. It
greets the early morning riders with a
10%
grade. Many riders quickly find
themselves with no remaining gears to
shift
into. As riders approach the first rest
stop, the skeleton squad delivers the
first pass sticker.
Riders replenish their fluids and choose
from a wide variety of food.
How you doing,
Jared? Smiles, everyone. Come on. It's
not that
bad. As riders crest the summit, they
are treated to majestic views of the
Sierra.
This pass is headquarters for the
CareFlight helicopter. Her crew is ready
at a moment's notice to assist anyone in
need of help. A testimony of the value
that the chamber places on rider
safety. Another thing these riders
appreciate after several miles of
climbing is the downhill.
At the Topaz Junction, the skeleton
squad awards the second pass sticker.
Bike mechanics are on hand to deal with
any necessary repairs. With no time to
waste, it's back on the
road. The final water stop on the east
side of Monitor Pass puts death riders
in right of passage
territory. If these guys don't get
riders pumped up, nothing
will.
Their valet style water service is like
no other.
These young men run down the hill to
meet thirsty riders, take their water
bottles, run back to the water station
to fill them, and runs them back to the
owner.
All the while they are cheering,
yelling, and encouraging the riders.
You can always hear the right of passage
group well before you see them.
Looking good out
there.
Their location is well situated because
shortly after passing by them, there's
about 5 miles of steep climbing ahead.
Let's go.
passion. Yes, sir. Let's go. Oh man,
this ride is great. I'm a little tired,
though. I was nervous last night, but it
turned out to be all right. I see why
why they call it the death ride, though.
[Applause]
As riders settle in for another tough
climb, it's back to the summit. Seeing
thousands of riders spread over several
miles is an awesome sight. The energy
from the camaraderie of these riders is
contagious as everyone is in the death
ride spirit.
Quite possibly the most thrilling
downhill on the entire course is the
west side of the path. Speeds can easily
reach 55 mph on the sweeping
turns.
Heat. Heat.
videos.curation_reason: Well-produced documentary with detailed course information and available transcript providing specific terrain details.

videos.curation_reason: Recent 2022 first-person race experience with good view count and compelling title comparing difficulty to Tour de France.

quotes.text: Monitor is a tough climb. It greets the early morning riders with a 10% grade. Many riders quickly find themselves with no remaining gears to shift into.

Their valet style water service is like no other. These young men run down the hill to meet thirsty riders, take their water bottles, run back to the water station to fill them, and runs them back to the owner. All the while they are cheering, yelling, and encouraging the riders.
Quite possibly the most thrilling downhill on the entire course is the west side of the pass. Speeds can easily reach 55 mph on the sweeping turns.

key_challenges.name: Monitor Pass Climb
key_challenges.description: A tough climb that greets early morning riders with a 10% grade. Many riders quickly find themselves with no remaining gears to shift into. The front and back side of Monitor Pass makes up approximately 28 miles of the course.

key_challenges.name: West Side Downhill
key_challenges.description: Quite possibly the most thrilling downhill on the entire course. Speeds can easily reach 55 mph on the sweeping turns.

key_challenges.name: 5 Miles of Steep Climbing
key_challenges.description: Shortly after passing the Right of Passage water station, there's about 5 miles of steep climbing ahead.

additional_quotes.text: Their valet style water service is like no other. These young men run down the hill to meet thirsty riders, take their water bottles, run back to the water station to fill them, and runs them back to the owner. All the while they are cheering, yelling, and encouraging the riders.
Her crew is ready at a moment's notice to assist anyone in need of help. A testimony of the value that the chamber places on rider safety.
The energy from the camaraderie of these riders is contagious as everyone is in the death ride spirit.

rider_intel.search_text: The Death Ride features Monitor Pass as a major challenge, with front and back sides making up approximately 28 miles of the course. The climb starts with a brutal 10% grade that leaves many riders with no remaining gears. Key locations include Highway 89 junction near Markleeville and Topaz Junction where riders receive pass stickers. The course offers incredible support with two water stops and two full rest stops on Monitor Pass alone. A CareFlight helicopter is stationed at the summit for safety. The famous Right of Passage water station provides energetic valet-style service before 5 miles of steep climbing. The west side descent is described as the most thrilling downhill, with speeds reaching 55 mph on sweeping turns. Bike mechanics are available at Topaz Junction for repairs.
